criteria: The agent has read Section 2 completely to understand the empirical analysis of prompt merits and the methodology used to discover them.
The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the construction of the merit-aligned prompt preference (POP) dataset.
The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand the DPO-based training objective for MePO.
The agent has read Section 4.1 completely to understand the experimental setup, datasets, baselines, and evaluation protocol.
The agent has read Table 2 to understand the performance comparison of MePO against BPO, FIPO, and inference-model-as-optimizer across multiple QA and math datasets.
The agent has read Table 3 to understand the win-rate evaluation results on instruction-following benchmarks.
The agent has read Appendix C (C.5, C.6) to understand the DPO input construction and the specific training hyperparameters.

The plan includes the merit discovery pipeline: sampling 5000 raw prompts from Alpaca, rewriting each 5 times with a lightweight LLM, scoring responses with DeepSeek-R1, and identifying merits from prompt pairs with score differences greater than 4.
The plan includes the construction of the POP dataset using Alpaca (30k) and BPO (13.96k) as base datasets with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct as the reference model for prompt optimization.
The plan includes the DPO training of MePO on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct with the specific hyperparameters: epochs=2, lr=1e-6, beta=0.01, sequence length=2048, loss type='sigmoid'.

The plan includes running experiments on ARC-Easy, ARC-Challenge, GSM8K, BBH, and PiQA datasets with evaluation on multiple inference models: Qwen2-7b, Tulu2-7b-dpo, LLaMA2-7b-chat-hf, Gemma2-9b, and Qwen3-8b.
The plan includes the ablation study removing each of the four merits (Clarity, Precision, Concise CoT, Preserve Original Information) one at a time.
The code implements the merit discovery process: loading 5000 random samples from the Alpaca dataset and prompting a lightweight LLM (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) to rewrite each prompt 5 times.
The code implements response scoring using DeepSeek-R1 to assess quality on a 0–10 scale, identifying pairs where the score difference exceeds 4 points as representing substantially better prompts.
The code implements a pairwise prompt comparison using DeepSeek-R1 to identify the merits (Clarity, Precision, Conciseness, etc.) that make higher-scoring prompts more effective.
The code identifies and formalizes the four prompt merits: (1) Clarity, (2) Precision, (3) Concise Chain-of-Thought, and (4) Preserve Original Information, matching the paper's definitions exactly.
The code implements loading Alpaca (instruction field as P_silver) and BPO (prompt field as P_silver) as base datasets for POP dataset construction.
The code implements merit-guided prompt optimization using Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct with the four-merit instruction template to generate P_golden from P_silver.
The code implements response generation from each P_golden (via Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) and retrieves R_silver from Alpaca's output and BPO's bad_res field.
The code implements the two filtering criteria for retaining 4-tuples: (1) R_golden receives a higher DeepSeek-R1 score than R_silver, and (2) the score of R_golden exceeds 8.

The code implements the prompt degradation procedure: randomly selecting 10% of P_silver from Alpaca and BPO datasets and intentionally degrading them using a base model to simulate noisy user inputs.

The constructed POP dataset produces approximately 40k 4-tuples (P_silver, P_golden, R_silver, R_golden), with the correct composition: ~25526 Alpaca Naive, ~3000 Alpaca Degraded, ~10225 BPO Naive, ~1400 BPO Degraded.

The DPO training input for each instance is formatted as a tuple (x, c, r) where x is the input prompt containing P_silver and R_silver, c is P_golden (chosen), and r is P_silver (rejected), consistent with the DPO objective in Equations (1)–(2).
The code implements DPO training with the sigmoid loss type, beta=0.01, learning rate=1e-6, per-GPU batch size=1, gradient accumulation steps=4, epoch=2, and sequence length=2048.
MePO is fine-tuned on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (not another model) using 4 NVIDIA RTX A5000 GPUs with input instructions truncated or padded to 2000 tokens.
The code loads optimization models (MePO, BPO, FIPO) in 8-bit precision and inference models in 4-bit mode with generation length below 512 tokens.
The evaluation protocol uses 3-shot for GSM8K, BBH, and PiQA, and zero-shot for ARC-Easy, ARC-Challenge, and instruction-following datasets.

Answer extraction uses a format-specific instruction approach with ## markers (e.g., 'Reply with the answer option starting with ##, like ##A, ##B, ##C, or ##D') and extracts the answer from model reasoning output rather than relying on next-token logit probabilities.
For BBH evaluation, all tasks are reformatted into multiple-choice format except multistep_arithmetic_two, object_counting, and word_sorting, which retain their original formats.
The baselines BPO (LLaMA2-7B-based, trained on GPT-3.5-optimized ChatGPT prompts) and FIPO (Tulu2-70B-DPO-based, trained on GPT-3.5/GPT-4-generated preference dataset) are implemented correctly.
The inference-model-as-optimizer baseline is implemented: each inference model is prompted using the format shown in Fig. 13 to rewrite its own prompts before generating responses.

The code implements evaluation on the instruction-following datasets (Vicuna Eval with 80 questions, BPO-Test Eval with 200 samples, Self-Instruct Eval with 252 instructions), using GPT-4o for win-rate scoring.
The training-free ablation baseline is implemented: the inference model rewrites raw prompts using static merit-guided templates (Fig. 16) without any training.
The merit ablation study implements four variants MePO_wo1–MePO_wo4, each removing one merit (Clarity, Precision, Concise CoT, Preserve Original Information) from the training-free template.
The downward compatibility analysis is implemented: evaluating MePO and baselines on lightweight inference models LLaMA3-8b-instruct, LLaMA3.2-3b-instruct, and LLaMA3.2-1b-instruct.
The upward compatibility analysis is implemented: evaluating MePO-optimized prompts on large-scale models LLaMA2-13b-chat-hf, LLaMA3.3-70b-instruct, and Tulu2-70b-dpo.
The MePO training pipeline executes successfully end-to-end: dataset construction, DPO fine-tuning of Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, and generation of the final MePO optimizer model.

The main experiments across ARC-Easy, ARC-Challenge, GSM8K, BBH, and PiQA on all five inference models (Qwen2-7b, Tulu2-7b-dpo, LLaMA2-7b-chat-hf, Gemma2-9b, Qwen3-8b) execute successfully without errors.
The instruction-following evaluation (Vicuna Eval, BPO-Test Eval, Self-Instruct Eval) across GPT-4o, DeepSeek-R1, Qwen-7B-Chat, and Vicuna-7B executes successfully.
The training-free baseline comparison experiments (Table 4) and merit ablation experiments (Table 5) execute successfully without errors.

The execution outcomes match Table 2: MePO achieves the highest average accuracy across all five inference models, outperforming BPO, FIPO, and the inference-model-as-optimizer baseline by approximately 1–3% on average.

The execution outcomes match Table 2: MePO achieves average accuracy gains of approximately 1.0% on Qwen2-7b, 2.37% on Tulu2-7b, 2.34% on LLaMA2-7b, 2.86% on Gemma2-9b, and 1.69% on Qwen3-8b compared to the best baseline.

The execution outcomes demonstrate MePO's robustness: BPO and FIPO show performance drops on Qwen3-8b compared to the inference-model baseline (BPO -0.8%, FIPO -3.97% on average), while MePO consistently outperforms the inference-model optimizer across all datasets.

The execution outcomes match Table 3: MePO achieves positive delta win rate (ΔWR) over all compared methods (unoptimized, inference model optimizer, BPO, FIPO) across Vicuna Eval, BPO-Test Eval, and Self-Instruct Eval for all four inference models.

The execution outcomes match Table 4: MePO outperforms the training-free merit-guided approach across all model backbones, with average accuracy improvements of approximately 1.01% on Qwen2, 2.38% on Tulu2, 2.53% on LLaMA2, 1.33% on Gemma2, and 0.6% on Qwen3.

The execution outcomes match Table 5: removing Clarity (MePO_wo1) and Precision (MePO_wo2) results in the largest performance drops, consistent with those merits being the most frequently identified by DeepSeek-R1 in Fig. 2.

The execution outcomes match Table 14: MePO shows consistent performance gains on lightweight inference models (LLaMA3-8b, LLaMA3.2-3b, LLaMA3.2-1b) while BPO and FIPO show notable performance degradation on these models.

The execution outcomes match Table 15: MePO prompts consistently match or improve performance on larger inference models (LLaMA2-13b, LLaMA3.3-70b, Tulu2-70b) across ARC, GSM8K, BBH, and PiQA datasets.
